title: Microscopic evidence for imaginary charge density wave in a kagome metal
authors: S. Suetsugu, F. Hori, M. Shibata, S. Kitagawa, K. Ishida, T. Asaba, S. Nakazawa, Q. Li, H. -H. Wen, T. Shibauchi, H. Kontani, Y. Matsuda
abstract: Dissipationless charge transport without any energy loss is one of the most fascinating phenomena in condensed matter physics. This extraordinary state manifests in two well-established systems: superconductors and quantum Hall systems. A proposed third category is associated with chiral loop current order, characterized by the spontaneous formation of microscopic electric current loops. The microscopic origin of these currents stems from imaginary hopping terms, conceptualized as an imaginary charge density wave (iCDW). Despite extensive investigations, its existence remains highly controversial. Here we report site-selective spectroscopic evidence for a pure iCDW in the kagome nonmagnetic metal CsV$_3$Sb$_5$. Nuclear quadrupole resonance spectra at out-of-plane $^{121}$Sb site sensitive to in-plane currents reveal anomalous broadening below $T^*\approx$120 K, coinciding with the nematic transition well above the real charge density wave (CDW). Under magnetic fields, the spectra exhibit asymmetric lineshapes, demonstrating that this broadening purely originates from magnetic effects rather than from electric quadrupolar effects associated with CDW fluctuations. The observed lineshapes are quantitatively consistent with ~1 mT local fields induced by chiral loop currents, indicating spontaneous time-reversal symmetry breaking. This microscopic identification of the long-sought pure iCDW establishes a novel form of quantum order, potentially revolutionizing our understanding of exotic electronic states in quantum materials.
summary: This paper reports the discovery of microscopic evidence for an imaginary charge density wave in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5. By using NMR/NQR spectroscopy, the authors identified spontaneous local magnetic fields arising from chiral loop currents. This establishes a new form of quantum order characterized by broken time-reversal symmetry.
main_problem: Determining whether the anomalous electronic phenomena in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 arise from real charge density waves or a controversial 'imaginary charge density wave' (iCDW) associated with spontaneous time-reversal symmetry breaking.
main_result: The study provides microscopic evidence for a pure iCDW state characterized by chiral loop currents, evidenced by spontaneous local magnetic fields of ~1 mT and a triple-q configuration.
method: Nuclear Quadrupole Resonance (NQR) and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy on the 121-Sb site, utilizing field-dependent and temperature-dependent measurements to disentangle magnetic and electric effects.
model_or_system: The kagome nonmagnetic metal CsV3Sb5, specifically focusing on the Sb1 and Sb2 sites within the 2D V-Sb kagome layers, modeled via a triple-q loop current configuration.
key_observables: Spectral linewidth (FWHM), spectral centroid asymmetry, local hyperfine field strength (h_loc), and transition frequency shifts.
important_parameters: Transition temperatures T_CDW (~94 K), T* (~120 K), and local magnetic fields (~1 mT).
main_assumptions: The assumption that the observed spectral asymmetry follows a triple-q distribution where sites experience hyperfine fields in a 1:3 ratio.
figures_summary: Figures show the temperature dependence of NQR linewidth broadening, the splitting of resonant frequencies under a c-axis magnetic field, and the successful fitting of experimental spectra using a triple-q model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the controversy of iCDW, presents NQR/NMR evidence of anomalous broadening at T*, demonstrates the magnetic origin of this broadening through field-dependent NMR, validates the triple-q model, and concludes with the identification of spontaneous time-reversal symmetry breaking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
